title: Upper Bound in a Sorted Array

content.problemStatement:
You are given a sorted array of integers numsnumsnums of size nnn. Your task is to find the **upper bound** of an integer xxx.

The **upper bound** of xxx is the **smallest index** i such that **nums[i] > x**. If no such index exists, return nnn (the size of the array).

Function DeclarationFunction NameupperBoundupperBoundupperBound – This function finds the upper bound index of a given value in a sorted array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A reference to a sorted array of integers.
xxx : The integer value whose upper bound is to be found.
Return Value
Returns the smallest index iii such that nums[i]>xnums[i] > xnums[i]>x
Returns nnn if no such index exists
The answers are taken in 0-based index. So return the value accordingly.
Constraints
1≤n≤1051 \leq n \leq 10^51≤n≤105
−105<nums[i],x<105-10^5 < nums[i], x < 10^5−105<nums[i],x<105
numsnumsnums is sorted in **ascending order**

Input Format

One line containing an integer nnn
One line containing nnn space-separated integers — the sorted array
One line containing an integer xxx


Output Format

Print a single integer — the upper bound index
constraints: nums is sorted in ascending order
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10^5), the size of the sorted array.
- A line with n space‑separated integers in non‑decreasing order (‑10^5 < nums[i] < 10^5).
- A line with integer x (‑10^5 < x < 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the smallest index i such that nums[i] > x. If no such index exists output n.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first element greater than 6 is 7 at index 3.
Elements equal to 4 are not greater; the first greater element is 6 at index 4.
All elements are greater than 0; the smallest index is 0.

solution.approach: Because the array is sorted we can locate the first element greater than x with binary search. Maintain two pointers low=0 and high=n. While low<high compute mid=(low+high)/2. If nums[mid] ≤ x, the answer must be to the right of mid, so set low=mid+1; otherwise set high=mid. When the loop ends low==high and equals the smallest index with nums[i]>x, or n if such element does not exist. This runs in O(log n) per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T·log n) – each test case performs a binary search on its array.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used besides the input array.
